coredata.dc:title: Controlled synthesis and photocatalytic performance of biocompatible uniform carbon quantum dots with microwave absorption capacity
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hanqi
Wang, Hong
Wang, Ying
Xin, Baifu
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully prepared via a facile microwave-assisted method combined with a controlled drainage time using glucose as the carbon source. XRD analysis, FT-IR spectroscopy, UV spectroscopy, XPS, and PL spectroscopy were performed to characterize the structures and properties of the samples. The results showed that the average particle size of the prepared CQDs was about 2 nm, and there were two UV absorption at the wavelengths of 228 nm and 280 nm, respectively, corresponding to the π-π* transition of CC (atomic group) and the n-π* transition of the oxygen containing group (CO) on the surface of the carbon core. According to the unique optical and microwave absorption properties of the CQDs, a suitable photocatalytic system, namely, a microwave electrodeless discharge lamp (MEDL) system was used. In the MEDL system, the degradation rate of rhodamine B (RhB, 50 mL, 30 mg L−1) by 0.02 g CQDs-2 in 10 min could reach 85.79%. The photocatalytic activity of CQDs-2 was about twice that of P25. In addition, the biocompatibility of the CQDs was investigated, which revealed that the CQDs could be absorbed by microorganisms as a carbon source. The prepared CQDs are a promising environmentally friendly photocatalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
Microwave
Photocatalytic
Biocompatibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of photocatalytic activity of CQDs
Fluorescence microscopy analysis of activated sludge

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program for Innovative Research Team in University
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Program for Jilin University Science and Technology Innovative Research Team

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by Program for Innovative Research Team in University ( IRT-1237 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of experimental apparatus (1. cooling system; 2. drainage control; 3. reaction solution; 4. microwave synthesis workstation). (b) Schematic of the synthesis of CQDs.
UV–vis spectra of CQDs. Inset: photograph of sample solutions under ambient light.
FT-IR spectra of CQDs and glucose.
C 1s XPS spectra of the as-prepared CQD samples: (a) CQDs-0, (b) CQDs-2, and (c) CQDs-4.

(a) TEM image of CQDs-2 assembled on an ultrathin carbon film. (b) HRTEM image of CQDs-2. Inset: the corresponding selected area FFT image. (c) Diameter distribution of CQDs (the red line is the Gaussian fitting curve) and (d) line profile of the selected CQD. (e,f) Topography and height analyses of CQDs-2 as shown in the AFM image. (Left) 2D image of CQDs. The labels A, B, and C are randomly selected. (Right) 3D image of CQDs.
(a) PL spectra of CQDs-0, CQDs-2, and CQDs-4 excited by 360 nm. (b) PL spectra of CQDs-2 in the excitation wavelength (λex) range of 320 nm to 500 nm.
Rate of degradation of RhB by CQDs in the MEDL system.

Fluorescence micrographs of CQDs and sludge cultured for different times under a blue filter. (a) CQDs; (b,c) pure sludge cultured for 0 h and 18 h; (d–f) sludge in contact with CQDs for 3, 7, and 18 h.
Comparison of (a) dehydrogenase activities and (b) protein contents of sludge with and without CQDs in sludge mixture.
Comparison of RhB photocatalytic degradation results of this study with those reported in literature.

row.entry: λ > 420 nm (300 W Xe Lamp with a UV-CUT Filter)
λ > 400 nm (150 W Xe Lamp with a UV-CUT Filter)
λ > 420 nm (500 W Xe Lamp with a UV-CUT Filter)

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: KCN80, the mass rate of KIT-6/Melamine is 80/100.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: m represents sample prepared by microwave-assisted synthesis method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: c represents sample prepared by the temperature-programed hydrothermal treatment synthesis method.

head.ce:title: Controlled synthesis and photocatalytic performance of biocompatible uniform carbon quantum dots with microwave absorption capacity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry
School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully prepared via a facile microwave-assisted method combined with a controlled drainage time using glucose as the carbon source. XRD analysis, FT-IR spectroscopy, UV spectroscopy, XPS, and PL spectroscopy were performed to characterize the structures and properties of the samples. The results showed that the average particle size of the prepared CQDs was about 2 nm, and there were two UV absorption at the wavelengths of 228 nm and 280 nm, respectively, corresponding to the π-π* transition of CC (atomic group) and the n-π* transition of the oxygen containing group (CO) on the surface of the carbon core. According to the unique optical and microwave absorption properties of the CQDs, a suitable photocatalytic system, namely, a microwave electrodeless discharge lamp (MEDL) system was used. In the MEDL system, the degradation rate of rhodamine B (RhB, 50 mL, 30 mg L−1) by 0.02 g CQDs-2 in 10 min could reach 85.79%. The photocatalytic activity of CQDs-2 was about twice that of P25. In addition, the biocompatibility of the CQDs was investigated, which revealed that the CQDs could be absorbed by microorganisms as a carbon source. The prepared CQDs are a promising environmentally friendly photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:para: Among the numerous unignorable environmental pollution crises, the effective purification of polluted water is the focus of worldwide attention. Synthetic dyes, Such as methylene blue (MB), malachite green (MG), methyl orange (MO), rose bengal (RB), RhB, and acid orange 7 dyes, are widely used in printing, textile, leather, cosmetic, dyeing, and petroleum industries. Due to improper treatment, a large number of dye wastewater can be produced, which will bring serious problems to the ecosystem and even lead to water related diseases [1–7]. To remove the dyes from wastewater, various conventional treatments such as advance oxidation [8], biological treatment [9], membrane technology [10], photocatalysis [6,11], and adsorption [1,2,7] have been utilized. All kinds of treatment processes have their own advantages and disadvantages. For example, adsorption can quickly remove dyes from water, but this way of physical transfer cannot completely solve the problem of environmental pollution [6]. Among these methods, photocatalysis driven by light energy has excellent performance in many aspects [6,12]. RhB is a common organic pollutant in triphenylmethane derivatives, which has numerous applications in manufacturing industry. When RhB wastewater with high organic content, high chroma and strong carcinogenicity is directly discharged into the natural drainage channel, many environmental problems will be caused, such as affecting the survival of aquatic organisms, even causing human death [11]. Therefore, it is worth studying to remove RhB dye from wastewater by effective treatment.

CQDs are novel zero-dimensional nanostructures that are less than 10 nm in size [13]. The photoluminescence (PL) of CQDs can be quenched effectively by electron donor or electron acceptor molecules in solutions. This indicates that photoexcited CQDs have excellent electron-transfer properties and can be used as an electronic medium, photosensitizers, photoluminescent materials, photoelectric converters, and photocatalysts [14–16]. In addition, CQDs are a small-molecule carbon material with excellent biocompatibility. Thus, they can also can be widely used in nanomedicine, biosensors, bioluminescence imaging, and other fields [13,17–19]. The application of CQDs, which combine the advantages of photocatalysis and biocompatibility, in environmental water treatment will enable not only the high-efficiency photocatalytic degradation of environmental pollutants, but also the degradation of the remaining photocatalysts by microorganisms, thus avoiding secondary pollution caused by the unrecovered catalysts. This would be an ideal way of treating environmental water. Unfortunately, the activity of CQDs is poor under general light, which limits their practical application [20].

As a carbon material, CQDs have strong microwave absorption ability [21,22]. When CQDs are exposed to microwave radiation, they interact strongly with the microwaves, resulting in many “hot spots” on their surfaces, where the temperature (easily exceeding 1000 °C) and energy are much higher than those of the other parts [22]. Undoubtedly, this will promote chemical reactions on the surface of CQDs. Furthermore, in the present work, microwave energy is used to excite a MEDL to emit multiple-wavelength, thus achieving photocatalysis. The MEDL system perfectly integrates the microwave/ultraviolet-visible illumination distinctive features [23,24,25]. This suggests that a MEDL catalytic system can be utilized for maximizing the catalytic degradation efficiency of CQDs.

The fabrication methods of CQDs can generally be classified into two approaches: “top-down” and “bottom-up” [14,17]. One of the preparation methods is the economical and facile microwave pyrolysis synthesis. It is widely regarded that catalysts with high degrees of crystallinity and high surface areas are desirable to enhance the photocatalytic activity [19,26,27]. The microwave method can effectively shorten the reaction time while providing homogeneous heating, which yields quantum dots with a uniform size [16,19]. The first goal of this work is to conveniently and controllably synthesize uniform ultrafine CQDs. This was achieved by a facile microwave-assisted method combined with a stage with controlled drainage time using glucose as the carbon source. Further, the as-prepared CQDs were applied in the MEDL catalytic system for a high-efficiency degradation of organic pollutants. The biodegradability of the CQDs was investigated, and the application prospects of the CQDs as an environmentally friendly photocatalyst are discussed.

All the chemicals were of analytical grade, except for tri(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (EP, USP, cell culture tested, ≥99.9%.), triphenyl tetrazolium chloride (TTC, biotechnology grade), bovine serum albumin (molecular biology grade), and Coomassie Brilliant Blue G-250 (CBBG-250; biotechnology grade). None of the reagents and chemicals was further purified.

A microwave atmospheric heating reflux apparatus with a steam outlet was used to rapidly prepare CQDs. A novel feature of the proposed method is that the water produced during the reaction process can be removed regularly, which allows controlling the reaction process. As the by-product water was discharged, the reaction temperature was gradually increased, thereby promoting the formation of a carbon core and the crystallization of the CQDs as well as increasing the yield of the CQDs.

A three-neck flask containing 15 g of glucose and 0.1 mL of deionized (DI) water was put into a microwave synthesizer (SINEO, MAS-II, China; Fig. 1 ). After the experimental parameters were set, the reaction was started. The experimental parameters were as follows: microwave power, 500 W; reaction temperature, 150 °C; reaction time, 7–11 min. After 7 min of reaction, the drainage valve (2) was opened, and the drainage time was controlled between 0 and 4 min. When the drainage time exceeded 4 min, the water in the reactor was completely drained; thus, the maximum reaction time was set as 4 min. During the reaction, the raw material changed from a solid powder to a liquid with an increase in heating time and drainage time, and finally changed to a highly viscous solid. The color of the solution changed from colorless to yellow and then to dark brown. The brown solution was dissolved in 30 mL of DI water and cooled to room temperature. The solution was put into a dialysis bag and purified (molecular weight: 3,000 Da) for 96 h. According to the different drainage times used during the syntheses, the prepared CQD samples were labelled as CQDs-0, CQDs-1, CQDs-2, CQDs-3, and CQDs-4.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained in the 2θ angle range of 10°–80° using a D8-advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, German) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15405 nm). All the samples were mixed with dry KBr and pressed into 1-mm-thick disks, which were analyzed using an FT-IR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer) in the wavenumber range of 4000–500 cm−1. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra of the aqueous solutions of CQDs and the degradation rate of RhB were analyzed using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV 8100A, LabTech). The size and morphology of the samples were determined by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker, Multimode 8) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2010) performed at 200 kV. The surface compositions and elemental chemical states of the samples were examined using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, Axis Ultra DLD) with Al Kα X-ray operated at 15 kV and 150 W. The binding energies (BEs) were calibrated with respect to the signal for adventitious carbon (BE = 284.6 eV), which provided BE values within an accuracy of ±0.1 eV. The PL spectra were recorded using a fluorescent spectrophotometer (FLS-1000, Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK). The fluorescence micrographs of the synthesized CQDs and activated sludge were obtained using a fluorescence microscope (Leica, Germany). A xenon lamp (PLS-SXE 300, Perfectlight, Beijing, China) was used as a simulated solar light source to test the photocatalytic activity of the samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of photocatalytic activity of CQDs

ce:section.ce:para: The MEDL photocatalytic reactor was composed of a microwave reactor and a MEDL (Fig. S1). A MEDL is a light source that emits light by absorbing microwave energy. The main radiations are 185, 253.7, 365, 436, 546, and 577 nm UV–vis light [28–30]. The MEDL used in the present work was made of quartz glass and filled with mercury and argon. It is cylindrical in shape (55 mm length and 14 mm diameter), designed according to the needs of the experimental device. The CQDs (0.02 g) and a 30 mg L−1 RhB solution (50 mL) were added to a 100 mL three-neck flask and stirred to form a homogeneous suspension. The suspension was stirred in the dark for 20 min to achieve an adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Then, the MEDL catalytic reaction was carried out for 10 min at 400 W of microwave power. At the end of the experiment, the absorbance of the RhB solution at 554 nm was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer.

The photocatalyst powder (0.03 g) was dispersed in 20 mL of a 10 mg L−1 RhB aqueous solution and stirred in the dark for 20 min to achieve an adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Then, the suspension was moved to the photocatalytic reactor with cooling circulating water. The distance between the xenon lamp and liquid surface was 7.5 cm. The xenon lamp was turned on to start the photocatalysis reaction, and samples were taken every 20 min to measure the absorbance using the UV–vis spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence microscopy analysis of activated sludge
ce:section.ce:para: A suitable amount of activated sludge was dried on a glass slide at room temperature, and fluorescence micrographs of the samples were taken using a fluorescence microscope with a blue filter. The fluorescence micrographs of the activated sludge samples were used to determine the changes in the contents of the intracellular fluorescent substances after the activated sludge was exposed to the CQDs for different times. A CQDs ethanol solution and the activated sludge without CQDs were used as blank controls.

Activated sludge contains bacteria, fungi, protozoa, and metazoa, which play a key role in decomposing organic matter and purifying sewage [31,32]. The effectiveness of sewage treatment depends largely on the activity of sludge. To detect the living microbes of sludge over time and ensure an efficient sewage treatment, the activated sludge can be characterized in terms of dehydrogenase activity [33,34], protein content [35,36], and sludge volume index (SVI) [37]. Therefore, in this study, the dehydrogenase activity and protein content were used as indicators to evaluate the effect of CQDs on the biological activity of activated sludge.

Activated sludge was activated and cultured before the experiment (the steps are described in the Supplementary Information). In this study, an experimental wastewater was self-formulated, which consisted of beer, FeSO4, KH2PO4, MgSO4, NH4Cl, CaCl2, and DI water [38]. The chemical oxygen demand (COD) of the experimental wastewater was 2300–2400 mg L−1, the pH was 6–7, and the concentrations of NH4-N and total phosphorus were 23 mg L−1 and 8.60 mg L−1, respectively. The trace elements were Fe (0.40 mg L−1), K (8.6 mg L−1), Mg (1.75 mg L−1), and Ca (6.13 mg L−1). The sludge was taken from a milk factory, and its mixed liquid suspended solids (MLSS) content was approximately 6.5–7.5 g L−1, SVI was 93–107 mL g−1, and color was yellow.

The activity of dehydrogenase was determined using the TTC method, as reported by Zheng and Jia [34]. First, 2 mL of an aerated sludge mixture, 3 mL of a Tris-HCl buffer solution, 1 mL of 0.36% Na2SO3 solution, and 4 mL of 0.4% TTC solution were sequentially added into a 50 mL centrifuge tube. The centrifuge tube was quickly put into a constant-temperature water bath oscillator (33 ± 1 °C) and the reaction was carried out for 30 min. After the reaction and 2 min of centrifugation at 10,000 rpm, the supernatant was discarded. Then, 10 mL of ethanol was added to the centrifuge tube, and the tube was shaken for 20 min in a constant-temperature water bath oscillator (33 ± 1 °C) to extract the red triphenyl formazan (TF). Finally, the supernatant containing TF was separated from the sludge via centrifugation for 2 min (10,000 rpm) and its absorbance at 485 nm was determined using a UV spectrophotometer. The above-mentioned operations were performed in the dark. The sludge was separated via centrifugation, dried at 60 °C, and its dry weight was measured. The dehydrogenase activity of TTC was calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
TTC
DHA
=
D 485
V
K
TTC
W t
where TTCDHA is the activity of the activated sludge dehydrogenase (μg mg−1h−1), D485 is the absorbance of the supernatant at 485 nm, V is the volume of the extractant (mL), KTTC is the slope of the standard curve (mL μg−1), W is the dry weight of activated sludge (mg), and t is the culture time of activated sludge dehydrogenase (h). The standard curve of TTC was shown in the Fig. S5a of Supplementary Information.

The concentration of protein in the extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) of sludge is an important index for determining the quality of activated sludge. In this study, the EPS were extracted by a heating method, and the protein content of the EPS of activated sludge was determined using the Coomassie Brilliant Blue method with bovine serum protein as the standard sample [35,36,38]. The extraction of EPS and determination of protein content are described in the Supplementary Information and the standard curve showed in Fig. S5b.

The crystal structures of the samples were characterized by XRD analysis. As can be seen in Fig. 2 , with increasing drainage time, the crystal size increased, as evident by the shifting of peaks to smaller 2θ angles. A wide peak in the 2θ angle range of 18.7°–22.5° (corresponding to the 002 crystal plane of graphite carbon) indicates that a graphite structure was formed [39–41]. The generation of the wide diffraction peak can be attributed to the small particle size, low crystallinity, and high dispersion of the CQDs prepared by the microwave-assisted drainage method. As determined by Scherrer’s equation, the crystal particle size is approximately 2 nm. Using Prague’s law, the spacing between the crystal planes was estimated as 0.39 nm.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the CQD samples in aqueous solutions are shown in Fig. 3 . The CQD samples prepared under different conditions have two distinct absorption peaks in the range of 230–320 nm. The absorption peak at 228 nm corresponds to the π-π* transition of CC (aromatic group), while the absorption peak at 280 nm corresponds to the n-π* transition of the oxygen-containing group (CO) on the surface of the carbon core. These results are consistent with those reported in literature [39,40,42,43]. The difference between the present study results and literature reports is that, in this work, the intensities of the absorption peaks at 228 and 280 nm increased gradually with increasing drainage time, which indicates that the contents of the oxygen-containing functional groups and crystalline graphene quantum units in the prepared sample gradually increased.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy is an effective method for detecting functional groups and determining covalent bond information. Fig. 4 shows the FT-IR spectra of raw glucose and the prepared CQD samples. The FT-IR spectra of the prepared samples are significantly different from that of raw glucose. Because the CQDs were formed via the drainage and condensation of glucose, the out-of-plane bending vibration of CH2 in the CQDs (at 779 cm−1) is much weaker than that of the CH2 bond in glucose. Moreover, the absorption peak at 1638 cm−1 in the CQD spectra indicates the presence of a CC bond (skeleton vibration of aromatic rings) [44]. The presence of these critical functional groups in the prepared samples indicates the formation of CQDs.

In addition, the CQD samples show significant peaks at 3411, 2928, 1364, 1076, and 1027 cm−1, which indicate that the prepared samples have the same functional groups and similar structures. The absorption peaks at 1364 cm−1 and 2928 cm−1 correspond to the angular vibration and stretching vibration of CH bonds, respectively [45,46]. The absorption peaks at 1027 cm−1 and 1076 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CO bonds [47], and the absorption peak at 3411 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of OH [48]. This indicates that the surface of the CQDs is highly hydrophilic. The OH groups ensure that the CQDs have good water solubility.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to determine the elemental composition of the CQDs and the chemical and electronic states of the elements. It was found that the CQDs are composed of C and O atoms (Fig. S2). Fig. 5 shows the C 1s spectra of CQDs-0, CQDs-2, and CQDs-4 after deconvolution using a Gauss–Lorentz function. The main peak line can be decomposed into six peaks, which are attributed to six functional groups on the surface of the CQDs, respectively.

Usually, the peak at a BE of 284.5 eV corresponds to CC (sp2) [44]. The central peaks at 286.6, 287.2, and 288.6 eV indicate the presence of COH, COC, and CO bonds, respectively [44,45,49]. The peak at 285.5 eV is characteristic of CH and CC bonds [44]. The XPS results for the surface functional groups are consistent with the FT-IR analysis results. Furthermore, Fig. 5 shows that the drainage time is directly related to the content of surface functional groups. The proportion of CC bond in the CQDs increased with increasing drainage time, which positively affected the crystal structure of the CQDs. Among the CQD samples, CQDs-2 has the highest sp2 C content. However, at drainage times of over 2 min, the proportion of CO also increased with increasing drainage time. The excessive CO groups will form recombination centers for the photogenerated carriers since the CO group is an electron-withdrawing group. This is unfavorable for the separation of electrons and holes, and reduces the photocatalyst activity.

The effect of drainage time on sample morphology was studied by analyzing the TEM images of the monodisperse CQDs-0 (Fig. S3a), CQDs-2 (Fig. 6 ), and CQDs-4 (Fig. S3b). The results show that the crystallinity and particle size of the CQDs increased with increasing drainage time. Among the CQD samples, CQDs-2 exhibited a high crystallinity and uniform particle size. Fig. 6a–d show that CQDs-2 has a spherical morphology with an average grain size of 2.23 nm. The lattice fringe structure can be clearly seen in the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image. The lattice spacing is 0.306 nm, which is consistent with the 002 planes of the graphite crystal and indicates that CQDs-2 has a hexagonal crystalline structure (Fig. 6b inset) [50,51]. The TEM results show that the CQDs prepared by the microwave method have uniform spherical particles with a small particle size. The TEM results are consistent with the XRD results.

AFM was performed to measure the particle thickness of the CQDs-2. As can be seen in Fig. 6e,f, the particle size of CQDs-2 synthesized by the microwave-assisted method with a controlled drainage stage is uniform (Rq = 0.417, Ra = 0.281). The height distribution of points A, B, and C in the figure shows that their thickness ranges from 2.35 to 3.03 nm, which is consistent with the TEM results. It is further proved that the prepared CQDs-2 has spherical particles of uniform size.

The PL properties of the CQDs are shown in Fig. 7 . As can be seen in Fig. 7a, the PL peak of CQDs-2 is significantly higher than that of CQDs-0, which is due to an increase in the content of aromatic sp2-hybridized carbon core with increasing drainage time during the preparation process; this improved the crystallinity of the CQDs and enhanced their fluorescence signals. However, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-4 weakened. This is because the amount of CO group on the surface of CQDs-4 increased. An appropriate amount of CO group is conducive to the separation of electrons and holes; however, when present in excess, the CO group acts as the recombination center for photogenerated carriers, leading to a decrease in fluorescence. Fig. 7b shows that the maximum emission wavelength shifted from 445 to 495 nm as the excitation wavelength changed from 320 to 500 nm. This indicates that the maximum emission wavelength of the CQD samples depends on the excitation wavelength, and confirms that the CQDs have abundant energy levels [52–54].

Fig. 8 shows the photocatalytic degradation rate of RhB over the prepared CQDs in the MEDL catalytic system. The degradation rate of RhB is only 13.75% in the MEDL without the CQDs. The photocatalytic degradation rate of P25 TiO2 photocatalyst is 47.98%. Obviously, the catalytic performance of P25 is much lower than that of the prepared CQD samples. As shown in Fig. 8, the rate of degradation of RhB by the as-prepared CQDs first increased and then decreased with increasing drainage time. At a drainage time of 2 min, the photocatalytic activity of the CQDs was the highest, and the degradation rate of RhB was 85.79%. Combined with the results of the visible-light catalysis experiment (Fig. S4), the photocatalytic efficiency of the CQDs under simulated sunlight was found to be only 14.99%, which is approximately one-seventh of that of P25. However, in the MEDL system, the photocatalytic performance of the CQDs is approximately twice that of P25. This can be attributed to the following:
(1)
The CQDs having abundant energy levels can absorb the multiple-wavelength light emitting from the MEDL to generate photoinduced carriers.
(2)
CQDs are excellent microwave-absorbing materials that can absorb microwave energy and generate many hot spots on their surfaces in the MEDL catalytic system. The photocatalytic experiment results suggest that the pyrolysis efficiency of RhB on the CQDs is poor (only about 3%); however, these hot spots can strongly promote the photocatalytic degradation reaction by elevating the interfacial reaction temperature. This is the main reason for the improved photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the CQDs. Obviously, the CQDs show a synergistic photocatalytic effect under light and microwave radiation in the MEDL catalytic system.
(3)
The CO group on the surface of the CQDs is an electron-withdrawing group, and its content increased gradually with increasing drainage time. An appropriate amount of CO group on the surface of the CQDs can effectively capture the photoinduced electrons, inhibiting the recombination of the photoinduced carriers and improving the photocatalytic activity of the CQDs. On the other hand, when present in excess, the CO groups on the surface of the CQDs act as recombination centers for the photoinduced electrons and holes, which is unfavorable for photocatalytic reactions.

According to the degradation experiment results, the CQDs can degrade the organic pollutant RhB in a very short time in MEDL system, which is an efficient photocatalyst. In order to further evaluate the outstanding degradation efficiency of the catalyst, the photocatalytic degradation experimental results of CQDs-2 were compared with the degradation efficiency of RhB organic dye by different photocatalysts, as shown in Table 1
[6,11,55–58]. It can be seen from Table 1 that the photocatalytic conversion rate of catalyst in MEDL system is generally higher than that under xenon lamp, while the CQDs obtained in this work has higher degradation efficiency and photocatalytic conversion rate compared with other catalysts in MEDL system. Its photocatalytic conversion is at least 5 times higher than that of other similar catalysts. Therefore, it is concluded that the CQDs is a promising and high-efficient photocatalyst suitable for MEDL system.

The CQDs are too small to be recovered after photocatalytic reaction, and the treatment solution will be directly discharged into the environmental water body. To examine the effect of CQDs on environment, biocompatibility experiments were performed.

Fig. 9 shows the fluorescence micrographs taken before and after the interaction of activated sludge with CQDs. Fig. 9a shows the fluorescence micrograph of pure CQDs used as a control. As can be seen, the pure CQDs emit yellow fluorescence. Fig. 9b,c show the fluorescence micrographs of activated sludge cultured for 0 and 18 h, respectively. As can be seen, the activated sludge (without CQDs) does not emit fluorescence. Fig. 9d–f shows the fluorescence micrographs of the activated sludge exposed to the CQDs for 3, 7, and 18 h, respectively. As can be seen, the intensity of yellow fluorescence emitted by the activated sludge microorganisms increased gradually with increasing culture time. This confirms that the CQDs become a part of the activated sludge cells and impart a fluorescence-emission ability to the activated sludge. This demonstrates that the CQDs can be absorbed and utilized by activated sludge as a carbon source when it comes in contact with the CQDs.

The effects of CQDs on the microbial activity of the activated sludge system were investigated by the TTC dehydrogenase method and Coomassie Brilliant Blue method [34,35]. The amount of CQDs in the biological experiments was the same as that in the photocatalytic experiments. Fig. 10 shows the effects of CQDs on the dehydrogenase activity and protein content of activated sludge. As can be seen, the dehydrogenase activity of activated sludge with the CQDs is good; specifically, it is higher than that of the control group without the CQDs. Furthermore, there is almost no difference in the protein contents of the EPS of activated sludge before and after the addition of CQDs. Based on the experimental results, it is concluded that the CQDs have no negative effect on activated sludge, and can be absorbed by microorganisms as a carbon source and improve the biological growth conditions, thus enhancing the biological activity of activated sludge. Thus, the CQDs have good biocompatibility.

Ultrafine CQDs were successfully synthesized from glucose and deionized water by a facile microwave-assisted method combined with a controlled drainage time. The XRD, FT-IR spectroscopy, UV spectroscopy, and XPS analyses results revealed that the CQDs have abundant functional groups on their surfaces and a particle size of approximately 2 nm. The oxygen-containing functional groups make the CQDs water-soluble. The PL spectra reveal that the prepared CQDs have a complex energy level structure, which enables the CQDs to absorb the multiple-wavelength light emitted from the MEDL to generate photoinduced carriers. The unique optical and microwave absorption properties of the CQDs endow them with high-efficiency photocatalytic activity to destroy the organic contaminants in a MEDL photocatalytic system. The possible reasons are follows: CQDs can absorb microwave energy and generate many hot spots on their surface in the MEDL catalytic system. These hot spots can strongly promote the photocatalytic degradation reaction by elevating the interfacial reaction temperature. Meanwhile, an appropriate amount of CO group with an electron-withdrawing capacity on the surface of the CQDs can effectively capture the photoinduced electrons, thereby inhibiting the combination of photoinduced carriers and improving the photocatalytic activity of the CQDs. The photocatalytic activity of the CQDs is approximately twice that of P25. In addition, the biocompatibility studies reveal that the CQDs can be absorbed and utilized by the microorganisms in activated sludge as a carbon source and have a positive effect on the activity of biological enzymes in activated sludge. In general, the prepared CQDs are a promising environmentally friendly photocatalyst.

Hanqi Zhang: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Hong Wang: Project administration, Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Ying Wang: Investigation. Baifu Xin: Funding acquisition, Resources, Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by Program for Innovative Research Team in University (IRT-1237).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.145751.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
